# Long-horizon benchmark results

*Committed snapshot of one run. Regenerate with*
`python scripts/run_long_horizon.py --seeds 5 --budgets 20000 100000 250000 --output results/long_horizon_benchmark`
*(writes tables, stats and figures to `results/long_horizon_benchmark/`, which is
gitignored). Follow-up to [`benchmark_results.md`](benchmark_results.md): only the
training budget changed — same algorithm, architecture, PPO settings, plasticity
coefficients and communication mechanisms.*

# Long-Horizon Benchmark — Does More Training Change the Result?

**Research question:** neuroplasticity may give no short-term benefit but could help at longer horizons. Only the training budget was changed (20k / 100k / 250k env steps) on **simple_spread**, comparing fixed fully-connected, adaptive and neuroplastic communication, **5 seeds** each. Algorithm, architecture, PPO settings, plasticity coefficients and communication mechanisms are unchanged. Higher return is better (rewards are negative).

> Still only a handful of seeds per cell — the permutation tests remain low-powered; read trends, not single p-values.

## Final reward by budget

| budget | fixed | adaptive | neuroplastic | Δ(plastic−fixed) | p vs fixed | p vs adaptive |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 20000 | -28.9 | -29.3 | -29 | -0.0998 | 0.845 | 0.713 |
| 100000 | -29.7 | -29.6 | -30.1 | -0.411 | 0.442 | 0.367 |
| 250000 | -28.5 | -28.1 | -28.7 | -0.259 | 0.546 | 0.268 |

## Does the communication graph change with budget?

- **20000 steps** (neuroplastic): edge-weight drift 0.00497, degree heterogeneity 0.0128, role diversity 0.146.
- **100000 steps** (neuroplastic): edge-weight drift 0.00294, degree heterogeneity 0.0175, role diversity 0.172.
- **250000 steps** (neuroplastic): edge-weight drift 0.00168, degree heterogeneity 0.199, role diversity 0.148.

## Outcome

**Outcome 3 — graph structure changes, reward does not.** The plastic graph keeps drifting/differentiating at every budget, but that never converts into a statistically significant reward advantage over fixed or adaptive communication.

## Was the earlier negative result caused by too little training?

**No.** Increasing the budget from 20000 to 250000 steps did not make neuroplastic communication significantly better than fixed or adaptive; the reward gap did not grow in neuroplastic's favour. The earlier negative result was **not** merely an artefact of short training — it persists at 12.5x the budget. (Absence of significance with few seeds is still not proof of no effect, but there is no positive trend to point to.)

## Limitations

- Single environment, one agent count (4); few seeds; no tuning. - 250k steps is still modest for MARL. - The plastic edge matrix is row-normalised, so its absolute magnitude cannot change aggregation beyond re-weighting existing neighbours.

_Auto-generated; the numbers above are the ground truth, favourable or not._

## Figures (in results/long_horizon_benchmark/)

- `final_reward_vs_budget.png` — all three methods overlap at every budget (no separation).
- `graph_heterogeneity_vs_budget.png` — the neuroplastic graph differentiates strongly with budget (0.013 → 0.20), far more than adaptive or fixed; the reward figure shows this does **not** convert into gains.
- `reward_curves_{20000,100000,250000}steps.png`, `communication_entropy_vs_budget.png`, `edge_weight_stability_vs_budget.png`, `convergence_vs_budget.png`, `reward_variance_vs_budget.png`, `specialisation_vs_budget.png`, `communication_heatmaps_250000steps.png`.
